items.title: Gravity near Earth
items.fact: Near Earth's surface, the acceleration due to gravity is about 9.8 m/s^2 downward. If a student asks for the force of gravity, clarify that force depends on mass.
items.formula: g = 9.8 m/s^2; weight force Fg = m × g
examples: A 10 kg object has weight Fg = 10 kg × 9.8 m/s^2 = 98 N.

items.title: Mass
items.fact: Mass is the amount of matter in an object. Mass is measured in grams or kilograms and does not change just because gravity changes.
items.formula: mass can be found from m = F/a or m = density × volume when those values are known

items.title: Weight
items.fact: Weight is the force of gravity pulling on an object. Weight changes when gravity changes and is measured in newtons.

items.title: Matter
items.fact: Matter is anything that has mass and takes up space.

items.title: Density
items.fact: Density tells how much mass is packed into a certain volume. Common units are g/mL, g/cm^3, or kg/m^3.
items.formula: density = mass / volume; D = m / V
examples: If mass = 20 g and volume = 5 cm^3, density = 20 / 5 = 4 g/cm^3.

items.title: Speed
items.fact: Speed tells how fast an object moves. It does not include direction.
items.formula: speed = distance / time; s = d / t
examples: If distance = 100 m and time = 20 s, speed = 5 m/s.

items.title: Velocity
items.fact: Velocity is speed in a specific direction. A car moving 20 m/s north has a velocity because direction is included.

items.title: Acceleration
items.fact: Acceleration is the rate that velocity changes over time. It can mean speeding up, slowing down, or changing direction.
items.formula: acceleration = change in velocity / time; a = Δv / t
examples: If velocity changes from 0 m/s to 20 m/s in 5 s, acceleration = 4 m/s^2.

items.title: Newton's Second Law
items.fact: Newton's Second Law says an object's acceleration depends on the net force on the object and the object's mass.
examples: If mass = 3 kg and acceleration = 2 m/s^2, net force = 6 N.

items.title: Newton's First Law
items.fact: Newton's First Law says an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at the same speed and direction, unless acted on by an unbalanced force.

items.title: Newton's Third Law
items.fact: Newton's Third Law says that for every action force, there is an equal and opposite reaction force.

items.title: Momentum
items.fact: Momentum describes how hard it is to stop a moving object. More mass or more velocity means more momentum.
examples: If mass = 4 kg and velocity = 3 m/s, momentum = 12 kg·m/s.

items.title: Kinetic Energy
items.fact: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has because it is moving.
examples: If mass = 2 kg and velocity = 3 m/s, KE = 0.5 × 2 × 9 = 9 J.

items.title: Gravitational Potential Energy
items.fact: Gravitational potential energy is stored energy based on an object's mass, gravity, and height.
examples: If mass = 2 kg, g = 9.8 m/s^2, and height = 5 m, GPE = 98 J.

items.title: Power
items.fact: Power is how quickly work is done or energy is transferred.
items.formula: power = work / time; P = W / t

items.title: Work
items.fact: In physics, work happens when a force moves an object over a distance in the direction of the force.
items.formula: work = force × distance; W = F × d

items.title: Ohm's Law
items.fact: Ohm's Law shows the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance in a circuit.
examples: If current = 2 A and resistance = 6 Ω, voltage = 12 V.

items.title: Atom
items.fact: An atom is the smallest unit of an element that still has that element's properties. Atoms contain protons, neutrons, and electrons.

items.title: Proton
items.fact: A proton is a positively charged particle in the nucleus of an atom. The number of protons tells the element's atomic number and identity.

items.title: Neutron
items.fact: A neutron is a particle in the nucleus with no electric charge. Protons plus neutrons make the mass number.

items.title: Electron
items.fact: An electron is a negatively charged particle found outside the nucleus. Valence electrons are the outer electrons involved in bonding.

items.title: Ion
items.fact: An ion is an atom or group of atoms with a charge because it gained or lost electrons. Losing electrons makes a positive ion; gaining electrons makes a negative ion.

items.title: Isotope
items.fact: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

items.title: Periodic Table Groups and Periods
items.fact: Groups are vertical columns on the periodic table. Periods are horizontal rows. Elements in the same group often have similar properties because they have the same number of valence electrons.

items.title: Common Element Quick Reference
items.fact: Common element symbols and atomic numbers: Hydrogen H=1, Helium He=2, Carbon C=6, Nitrogen N=7, Oxygen O=8, Sodium Na=11, Chlorine Cl=17, Iron Fe=26, Copper Cu=29, Silver Ag=47, Gold Au=79, Lead Pb=82.
